title: Needle-felted horse
subtitle: A standing chestnut horse, approximately 18 cm tall, needle-felted over a wire armature with a flowing mane and tail
excerpt: A standing horse figure in chestnut merino, approximately 18 cm tall, built over a wire armature. The mane and tail are made from unspun raw fleece locks or mohair locks for a realistic flowing texture. Proportions follow the warmblood type: deep chest, long neck, and well-angled hindquarters.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted horse sculpture technique synthesised from general armature-based animal needle-felting practice. Equine anatomy based on standard warmblood proportions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A wire skeleton that supports the roving layers of a needle-felted sculpture, allowing the piece to hold a standing or posed position.
Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope used as the raw material for needle felting.
Unprocessed locks of Angora goat fibre, used in needle felting to create flowing hair textures for manes, tails, and fleece effects.
A felting needle with small notches along the shaft that tangle fibres together by mechanical action when pushed in and pulled out.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

content.text: A needle-felted horse is a demanding figure. The proportions are distinctive and hard to disguise if wrong: a horse's neck is long relative to its body, the legs are long and slender, and the hooves are small dense cylinders. The
armature
must be sturdy enough to hold the weight of the
roving
without bending. The mane and tail are the visual payoff:
mohair locks
or long raw fleece locks give a flowing texture that needle-felted merino alone cannot replicate.

items.name: Merino roving, chestnut or bay colour
Mohair locks or long raw fleece locks, black or dark brown (mane and tail)
Merino roving, black (hooves and lower legs)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Build the armature

Cut a 70 cm length of 2 mm wire for the four legs and spine. Bend at the centre for the front legs (each approximately 9 cm). Twist the central section into a double strand for the spine (approximately 12 cm). Extend into the rear legs (each approximately 10 cm). Bend a flat loop at the base of each leg to form the hoof area. Cut a separate 30 cm piece for the neck and head: bend into a long curve ending in a flattened loop for the muzzle. Attach the neck wire to the top of the spine using a tight spiral of thinner wire. Use pliers to ensure all joins are firm before adding any roving.
Step 2: Pad the body
Wrap Corriedale core wool around the spine section, building the barrel of the chest and the hindquarters. The chest should be deeper than it is wide: horses are narrow animals. Use a size 36
barbed needle

to compact the core wool firmly before adding any surface roving. The hindquarters need particular attention: add extra Corriedale to build the rounded muscular mass of the hindquarters above the rear legs.
Step 3: Cover with chestnut merino

Using a size 38 needle, stab short tufts of chestnut merino over the core body in overlapping rows, working from the hindquarters forward. Cover the neck in the same way, building the characteristic crest of the mane area with slightly longer roving pushed in at an angle. Cover each upper leg with chestnut merino wound tightly. Work black merino into the lower legs and over the hoof loops for the dark points of a bay-coloured horse.
Step 4: Build the head

Build the head separately around the neck wire loop: wrap chestnut merino around the flattened muzzle loop and compact with a size 36 needle. The head should be approximately 5 cm from poll to muzzle. Work a white blaze down the face with fine white merino and a size 40 needle. Attach glass bead eyes or needle-felt dark brown circles for the eyes. The ears are small pointed cones of chestnut merino, needle-felted to firmness and stabbed into position on the crown.
Step 5: Add the mane and tail

Take small bundles of mohair locks (2-3 locks each time) and lay them along the crest of the neck. Using a size 38 needle, stab the root end of each bundle into the roving firmly, leaving the locks to hang freely. Work from the poll to the withers, overlapping each bundle slightly. The tail is attached at the top of the hindquarters: bind a larger bundle of locks with a length of thread, stab the bound end firmly into the rump area, and allow the locks to hang naturally.

items.problem: The legs splay outward and the horse will not stand without support
items.cause: The hoof-pad loops were not made firmly, or the leg wire is too thin relative to the body weight
items.fix: Add a second wire to each leg alongside the existing one and compact the hoof area with a thick pad of black merino. The flat base of the hoof must be perpendicular to the floor.

items.problem: The neck droops forward when the figure is unsupported
items.cause: The neck wire is too thin or not firmly attached to the spine section
items.fix: Reinforce the neck-to-spine join with an additional wrap of wire and add extra core wool to the upper chest and lower neck to support the angle.

items.problem: The mohair mane locks pull out when touched
items.cause: The root of each bundle was not stabbed deeply enough into the underlying roving
items.fix: Stab the root end of each lock bundle through the surface merino and into the core wool using firm, repeated stabs with a size 38 needle. The lock must be compacted into the underlying layer, not just sitting on the surface.

content.text: Finishing

Trim stray fibres with small scissors. The mane can be trimmed to a pulled or plaited style or left natural. White merino stabbed above the hoof loops creates the sock markings typical of many breeds. The finished figure can stand on its own four hooves if the armature and hoof pads are properly constructed.
